title: Rigidity and Flexibility of Periodic Hamiltonian Flows
speaker: Gabriele Benedetti
abstract: An old problem in classical mechanics is the existence of periodic flows within specific classes of Hamiltonian systems such as geodesic and magnetic flows, and central forces. In the last years, interest in this problem has been revitalized since recent research has unveiled a deep relationship between periodic Hamiltonian flows and systolic questions in symplectic and contact geometry. While only trivial examples of periodic flows among magnetic and central systems exist, Zoll and, later, Guillemin have shown that there are many exotic examples among geodesic flows on the two-sphere.

proof_stages.label: Hamiltonian models for periodic-flow questions
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up the main classes of Hamiltonian systems under consideration—central-force systems, geodesic flows, and magnetic geodesic flows—and explains the twisted symplectic formulation of the magnetic case.

proof_stages.label: Zoll condition and classical benchmarks
proof_stages.summary: A periodic Hamiltonian energy level is specified to mean a free S^1-action by the flow, excluding merely Besse behavior, and the standard classical Zoll systems are recalled as benchmarks for the rigidity-versus-flexibility question.

proof_stages.label: Systolic motivation for Zoll hypersurfaces
proof_stages.summary: For contact-type energy hypersurfaces, the systolic ratio is defined and Zoll hypersurfaces are presented as local maximizers, motivating the search for and classification of Zoll Hamiltonian levels.

proof_stages.label: Rigidity mechanisms
proof_stages.summary: The speaker surveys rigidity results showing that periodicity across sufficiently many levels, or on RP^2, forces the system back to the classical models: Bertrand’s theorem, Green’s theorem, and a low-energy magnetic rigidity theorem.

proof_stages.label: Sphere flexibility and Radon-linearization paradigm
proof_stages.summary: Guillemin’s theorem for conformal Zoll deformations of the round S^2 is recalled, with odd functions characterized as the kernel of the great-circle Radon transform, which is interpreted as the linearization of the action functional.

proof_stages.label: Magnetic torus setup and linearized transform
proof_stages.summary: For magnetic flows on T^2 with metric and magnetic field depending only on one coordinate, the data are normalized as a pair of zero-average functions, a magnetic Radon-type transform R_h is defined by boundary and disk integrals over the unperturbed circular orbits, and the main theorem states that every kernel direction integrates to a Zoll deformation at the fixed energy h.

proof_stages.label: Implicit-function proof with derivative-loss repairs
proof_stages.summary: The proof constructs a nonlinear action map via finite-dimensional reduction so that vanishing corresponds to Zollness, then overcomes non-C1 derivative loss by Nash–Moser ideas and a normal-operator argument that restores enough surjectivity in a neighborhood.

examples.name: Surface magnetic geodesics as prescribed-curvature curves
examples.note: For a two-dimensional configuration manifold, the magnetic equation is recast as curves with prescribed geodesic curvature, making the magnetic-flow setup geometrically concrete without being used in the proof.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker specializes magnetic geodesics to surfaces and derives the prescribed-curvature equation k_g = f(q)/sqrt(2h) for energy h

examples.note: Convex hypersurfaces in Euclidean symplectic space are mentioned as a setting where the systolic ratio can be viewed as a capacity, illustrating why Zoll hypersurfaces matter for systolic questions.

examples.name: Zoll metrics of revolution
examples.note: Zoll’s explicit metrics of revolution on the sphere are cited as a concrete historical manifestation of the flexibility theorem for geodesic flows on S^2.
examples.engagement_reason: Zoll metrics of revolution are cited as Zoll’s explicit construction but no construction or verification is carried out

passes.reason: Zoll metrics of revolution are rotationally symmetric Zoll systems and hence local systolic extremal examples, used constructively for flexibility.
Zoll metrics of revolution are rotationally regular Zoll metrics that realize the Zoll/systolic extremal condition and show non-round S2 Zoll flexibility.
Zoll metrics of revolution are rotationally organized Zoll examples whose all-geodesics-closed property makes them local systolic extremizers, introduced as positive flexible constructions.
Zoll metrics of revolution form a rotationally regular all-geodesics-closed family, hence local systolic extremals, and are used constructively as flexibility examples.
Zoll metrics of revolution are highly regular rotational Zoll metrics and serve as non-round examples showing flexibility on S^2 rather than round-metric rigidity.
Zoll metrics of revolution are rotationally regular Zoll examples whose closed-geodesic flow is locally systolic-optimal, but they are presented constructively rather than as a refutation.
Non-round Zoll metrics of revolution exhibit S2 flexibility against round-only rigidity, while Zollness makes them local systolic extremals and the revolution assumption gives rotational symmetry.
Zoll metrics of revolution give non-round all-geodesics-closed metrics on S^2, showing rigidity beyond the standard metric fails; Zoll systems are systolic extremals.
Zoll metrics of revolution are rotationally regular positive examples and, being Zoll, lie on the local systolic-maximizing/equality side.
Zoll metrics of revolution form a rotationally regular family whose Zoll property makes them equality/local-maximizer cases for the relevant systolic ratio, and they are not used adversarially.

examples.name: Ellipsoid-induced Besse example on S^2
examples.note: In response to a question about excluding Besse behavior, the speaker contrasts the torus construction with an S^2 example related to an ellipsoid where a Besse, non-Zoll situation can occur.
examples.engagement_reason: the S^2 Besse example from conjugating with an ellipsoid is only described briefly as a standard-ellipsoid disguise

passes.reason: The ellipsoid-induced Besse example is cited to show that on S^2 a periodic flow can be Besse rather than Zoll.
The ellipsoid-induced S2 flow is a symmetric Besse-but-not-Zoll example used to show that periodic Besse behavior can occur without the free Zoll action.
The ellipsoid-induced Besse flow is a highly regular ellipsoidal example with periodic but non-free dynamics, used to show that on S^2 periodicity need not imply Zoll.
The ellipsoid-induced Besse flow is a symmetric periodic model used as a periodic-but-not-Zoll case showing that Besse behavior can occur on S^2.

The ellipsoid-induced rational Besse flow is a highly regular toric model cited as a Besse-not-Zoll enemy case on S^2.
